model_narrative: A pipe network must be laid out to collect production from a set of nodes and route it to a single port. For each directed connection between two nodes the model decides whether to lay a baseline pipe and, on top of that baseline, whether to add a single capacity upgrade. It also decides how much flow runs along each connection. The goal is to choose the cheapest set of pipes and upgrades that can carry every node's production to the port, where the cost of any pipe scales with the distance it spans.

sets.name: n
sets.doc: nodes in the network

sets.name: k
sets.doc: pipe types; type 2 is the baseline and the rest are larger

sets.name: kk
sets.doc: upgrade pipe types, that is the pipe types larger than the baseline

sets.name: port
sets.doc: the sink node that absorbs all collected production

sets.name: regnode
sets.doc: flow-conserving nodes, that is every node except the port

sets.name: nw
sets.doc: nodes that may originate a pipe

sets.name: arc
sets.doc: directed arcs (i, j); both orientations of every physical edge are present

params.name: cap
params.doc: incremental flow capacity of a pipe type, measured as the extra capacity it adds over the baseline; the baseline type itself carries the baseline capacity

params.name: pipecost
params.doc: incremental cost per unit distance of a pipe type, measured as the extra cost it adds over the baseline

params.name: cap1
params.doc: the baseline pipe capacity that any built pipe provides

params.name: pipecost1
params.doc: the baseline pipe cost per unit distance

params.name: p
params.doc: production at a node; a node with zero production is a pass-through node, a node with positive production is a source

params.name: dist
params.doc: length of an arc between two nodes

vars.name: b
vars.doc: 1 if a baseline pipe is laid on the arc, else 0

vars.name: bk
vars.doc: 1 if the given upgrade type is added on top of the baseline pipe on the arc, else 0

vars.name: f
vars.doc: flow sent along the arc

vars.name: cost
vars.doc: total installation cost of the chosen network

constraints.name: cost
constraints.intent: Define the total installation cost as the sum over every arc that starts at a node able to originate a pipe of the arc's length multiplied by the cost of whatever is built on it, where a built baseline pipe contributes its baseline cost and each added upgrade contributes its own incremental cost.

constraints.name: p[i])
constraints.intent: At each pass-through node that has no production, allow at most one baseline pipe to leave the node. Skip this requirement at any node that has production and at any node with no outgoing arcs.

constraints.name: p[i])
constraints.intent: At each node that has production, require exactly one baseline pipe to leave the node so its output has a single starting direction. Skip this requirement at any node with no production and at any node with no outgoing arcs.

constraints.name: regnode:
constraints.intent: At every flow-conserving node, the node's own production must equal the total flow leaving it minus the total flow entering it, so production plus whatever arrives is exactly what departs. Skip this at the port, which simply absorbs all the collected production.

constraints.name: nw:
constraints.intent: On every arc leaving a node that can originate a pipe, the capacity installed there must be enough to carry the flow on that arc, where the installed capacity is the baseline capacity of a built pipe plus the extra capacity contributed by any upgrade added to it.

constraints.name: nw:
constraints.intent: On every arc leaving a node that can originate a pipe, an upgrade may only be added where a baseline pipe is actually built, and since only one upgrade can ever be present the total number of upgrades on the arc can be at most one when a baseline pipe is laid and none otherwise.

query: Permitting is a headache, so we're looking at a leaner layout that lays no more than 28 pipe segments in total. What does the network cost come to under that cap?
expected_pyomo: model.cap_pipes = Constraint(expr=sum(model.b[a] for a in model.arc) <= 28)

query: Engineering is pitching a no-dead-end routing principle: any time we feed oil into the node-3 collector by laying the 1-to-3 segment, we should also commit to the onward leg out of node 3 toward node 4 so the flow keeps moving. Build that linkage in and tell me what the network ends up costing.

query: There's a proposal on the table to hold the build to a much leaner footprint — no more than roughly 22.5 percent of all the candidate corridors in the network should carry pipe. Apply that ceiling on the overall share of corridors used and tell me the resulting construction cost.
expected_pyomo: model.cap_total = Constraint(expr=sum(model.b[a] for a in model.arc) <= 0.225*len(model.arc))

query: The board is pitching a combined scenario: keep the overall build lean by holding pipe to at most roughly 22.5 percent of all candidate corridors, and at the same time guarantee that whenever we feed the node-3 collector by laying the 1-to-3 segment, we also lay the onward leg out of node 3. Run both of those together and tell me the resulting cost.
expected_pyomo:
model.cap_total = Constraint(expr=sum(model.b[a] for a in model.arc) <= 0.225*len(model.arc))
model.feed_onward = Constraint(expr=model.b['3', '4'] >= model.b['1', '3'])

query: Several of the collector junctions look heavily over-fed — the plan piles a lot of inbound pipes onto them. Shouldn't each node's inbound pipe count stay under roughly half of the corridors that could physically feed it, so no single junction gets overloaded? What's the model seeing that I'm not?
expected_pyomo:
def _half_inflow(model, n): return sum(model.b[a] for a in model.arc if a[1] == n) <= 0.5*len([a for a in model.arc if a[1] == n]) if len([a for a in model.arc if a[1] == n]) >= 2 else Constraint.Skip
model.half_inflow = Constraint(model.n, rule=_half_inflow)

query: The plan keeps building out a lot of corridors — it feels like we're laying pipe across far more of the network than we should need. Couldn't we hold the build to something tighter, no more than about 22 percent of all the candidate corridors carrying pipe? What's keeping the model from making that move?
expected_pyomo: model.cap_total = Constraint(expr=sum(model.b[a] for a in model.arc) <= 0.22*len(model.arc))

query: Some of the collector nodes still look saturated even after a light trim — the design crowds inbound pipes onto them. Wouldn't it be more disciplined to hold each node's inbound pipe count down to no more than about two-fifths of the corridors that could feed it? What's pulling the design toward packing them so tightly?
expected_pyomo:
def _lean_inflow(model, n): return sum(model.b[a] for a in model.arc if a[1] == n) <= 0.4*len([a for a in model.arc if a[1] == n]) if len([a for a in model.arc if a[1] == n]) >= 2 else Constraint.Skip
model.lean_inflow = Constraint(model.n, rule=_lean_inflow)

vars.name: cost
vars.doc: total installation cost of the chosen network

constraints.name: cost
constraints.intent: Define the total installation cost as the sum over every arc that starts at a node able to originate a pipe of the arc's length multiplied by the cost of whatever is built on it, where a built baseline pipe contributes its baseline cost and each added upgrade contributes its own incremental cost.

constraints.name: p[i])
constraints.intent: At each pass-through node that has no production, allow at most one baseline pipe to leave the node. Skip this requirement at any node that has production and at any node with no outgoing arcs.

constraints.name: p[i])
constraints.intent: At each node that has production, require exactly one baseline pipe to leave the node so its output has a single starting direction. Skip this requirement at any node with no production and at any node with no outgoing arcs.

constraints.name: regnode:
constraints.intent: At every flow-conserving node, the node's own production must equal the total flow leaving it minus the total flow entering it, so production plus whatever arrives is exactly what departs. Skip this at the port, which simply absorbs all the collected production.

constraints.name: nw:
constraints.intent: On every arc leaving a node that can originate a pipe, the capacity installed there must be enough to carry the flow on that arc, where the installed capacity is the baseline capacity of a built pipe plus the extra capacity contributed by any upgrade added to it.

constraints.name: nw:
constraints.intent: On every arc leaving a node that can originate a pipe, an upgrade may only be added where a baseline pipe is actually built, and since only one upgrade can ever be present the total number of upgrades on the arc can be at most one when a baseline pipe is laid and none otherwise.

query: Two things about the layout bother me at once: the collector junctions get crowded with inbound pipe, and at the node-3 collector we push oil in by laying the 1-to-3 segment without committing the onward leg that carries it forward. Couldn't we both hold each node's inbound pipes to at most half of its feasible corridors and insist the onward leg out of node 3 exists whenever node 3 is fed? What's the tradeoff that's keeping the design from doing both?
expected_pyomo:
def _half_inflow(model, n): return sum(model.b[a] for a in model.arc if a[1] == n) <= 0.5*len([a for a in model.arc if a[1] == n]) if len([a for a in model.arc if a[1] == n]) >= 2 else Constraint.Skip
model.half_inflow = Constraint(model.n, rule=_half_inflow)
model.feed_onward = Constraint(expr=model.b['3', '4'] >= model.b['1', '3'])

model_narrative: A pipe network must be laid out to collect production from a set of nodes and route it to a single port. For each directed connection between two nodes the model decides whether to lay a baseline pipe and, on top of that baseline, whether to add a single capacity upgrade. It also decides how much flow runs along each connection. The goal is to choose the cheapest set of pipes and upgrades that can carry every node's production to the port, where the cost of any pipe scales with the distance it spans.

sets.name: n
sets.doc: nodes in the network

sets.name: k
sets.doc: pipe types; type 2 is the baseline and the rest are larger

sets.name: kk
sets.doc: upgrade pipe types, that is the pipe types larger than the baseline

sets.name: port
sets.doc: the sink node that absorbs all collected production

sets.name: regnode
sets.doc: flow-conserving nodes, that is every node except the port

sets.name: nw
sets.doc: nodes that may originate a pipe

sets.name: arc
sets.doc: directed arcs (i, j); both orientations of every physical edge are present

params.name: cap
params.doc: incremental flow capacity of a pipe type, measured as the extra capacity it adds over the baseline; the baseline type itself carries the baseline capacity

params.name: pipecost
params.doc: incremental cost per unit distance of a pipe type, measured as the extra cost it adds over the baseline

params.name: cap1
params.doc: the baseline pipe capacity that any built pipe provides

params.name: pipecost1
params.doc: the baseline pipe cost per unit distance

params.name: p
params.doc: production at a node; a node with zero production is a pass-through node, a node with positive production is a source

params.name: dist
params.doc: length of an arc between two nodes

vars.name: b
vars.doc: 1 if a baseline pipe is laid on the arc, else 0

vars.name: bk
vars.doc: 1 if the given upgrade type is added on top of the baseline pipe on the arc, else 0

vars.name: f
vars.doc: flow sent along the arc

vars.name: cost
vars.doc: total installation cost of the chosen network

constraints.name: cost
constraints.intent: Define the total installation cost as the sum over every arc that starts at a node able to originate a pipe of the arc's length multiplied by the cost of whatever is built on it, where a built baseline pipe contributes its baseline cost and each added upgrade contributes its own incremental cost.

constraints.name: p[i])
constraints.intent: At each pass-through node that has no production, allow at most one baseline pipe to leave the node. Skip this requirement at any node that has production and at any node with no outgoing arcs.

constraints.name: p[i])
constraints.intent: At each node that has production, require exactly one baseline pipe to leave the node so its output has a single starting direction. Skip this requirement at any node with no production and at any node with no outgoing arcs.

constraints.name: regnode:
constraints.intent: At every flow-conserving node, the node's own production must equal the total flow leaving it minus the total flow entering it, so production plus whatever arrives is exactly what departs. Skip this at the port, which simply absorbs all the collected production.

constraints.name: nw:
constraints.intent: On every arc leaving a node that can originate a pipe, the capacity installed there must be enough to carry the flow on that arc, where the installed capacity is the baseline capacity of a built pipe plus the extra capacity contributed by any upgrade added to it.

constraints.name: nw:
constraints.intent: On every arc leaving a node that can originate a pipe, an upgrade may only be added where a baseline pipe is actually built, and since only one upgrade can ever be present the total number of upgrades on the arc can be at most one when a baseline pipe is laid and none otherwise.

query: Why fuss over sizing each segment individually? Just slap the biggest pipe type on every arc we build and be done with it. How much does that one-size-fits-all plan overspend versus the optimized design?
expected_pyomo:
model.heur6 = Constraint(model.arc, rule=lambda m, i, j: m.bk[i, j, max(m.kk, key=int)] == m.b[i, j])
model.heur_others = Constraint(model.arc, model.kk, rule=lambda m, i, j, k: Constraint.Skip if k == max(m.kk, key=int) else m.bk[i, j, k] == 0)
